title: Gaps and Holes in Latent Space
concept: Vanilla autoencoders only learn to decode the exact points seen during training, leaving large unexplored gaps between them that decode to garbage.

exercises.prompt: Imagine a map with a few marked cities but no roads drawn between them. If you land in the empty space between cities, do you know where you are?

options.text: No — that empty space isn't defined by anything on the map
Yes — the empty space is automatically another city
Yes — empty space always turns into a road

exercises.explain: Just like blank map space isn't a real place, the empty gaps in latent space aren't taught to mean anything!

cards.title: The Space Between the Dots
cards.body: A vanilla autoencoder learns to perfectly rebuild the exact pictures it was trained on. But what happens if you pick a point it never saw — right in the gap between two training examples? Often... total garbage. Let's find out why!

cards.title: What Are Latent Space Gaps?
cards.body: A vanilla autoencoder squishes each training example into a single point in 'latent space' (its compressed code). It only learns what those exact points should decode into. Nobody ever taught the decoder what to do with the empty areas between those points — so those areas are just gaps and holes with no real meaning.

cards.title: Paint-by-Numbers Gone Wrong
cards.body: Imagine a paint-by-numbers picture where only a few numbered dots have paint on them, and the rest of the canvas is blank. If someone asks 'what color is the blank spot between dot 3 and dot 7?' — there's no answer! Nobody painted that spot or told you what belongs there. That's exactly what happens when a vanilla autoencoder's decoder faces an unexplored gap.

cards.title: Cat, Dog... or Neither?
cards.body: Say the encoder places 'cat' pictures at one point in latent space and 'dog' pictures at another point far away. If you pick the exact midpoint and ask the decoder to reconstruct it, you might expect a cute cat-dog blend. Instead, since the decoder never learned that midpoint, you often get a blurry, meaningless smear — not cat, not dog, just noise.

cards.title: Myth: More Compression Fixes Gaps
cards.body: Myth: If we just squeeze the latent space smaller, the gaps disappear. Truth: Squeezing doesn't teach the decoder new meanings for empty regions — the gaps just get smaller but are still undefined. The real fix is different training methods (like variational autoencoders) that force the space to be smooth and continuous, not just squished.

cards.title: Filled Map vs. Dotted Map
cards.body: Compare a latent space that's been trained to fill in every region smoothly against one that only knows scattered points.

left.text: Only isolated points decode well; everything between them is an undefined hole that produces garbage output

right.text: Every point, even ones never seen in training, decodes into something meaningful and realistic

exercises.prompt: Why do gaps exist between points in a vanilla autoencoder's latent space?

options.text: The decoder was only ever trained on specific example points, not the space between them
The computer runs out of memory in those areas
Gaps are added on purpose to save storage space

exercises.explain: The decoder only learns meaning for the exact points it saw during training — the untouched space in between stays undefined.

exercises.prompt: If you pick a totally new point between two training examples in a vanilla autoencoder's latent space, the decoder will always produce a perfect blend of the two examples.
exercises.explain: False! Since that in-between point was never trained on, the decoder often produces blurry, meaningless garbage instead of a clean blend.

exercises.sentence: A vanilla autoencoder only learns to decode the ____ points it saw during training, leaving the space between them undefined.
exercises.explain: The decoder is only trained on the exact points from the training data — everything else is a mysterious gap.

exercises.prompt: A friend says: 'Since our autoencoder can perfectly rebuild all our training pictures, it must also generate great new pictures from any random point in latent space.' What's the flaw in this thinking?

options.text: Perfectly rebuilding trained points says nothing about the huge undefined gaps between them, which usually decode to garbage
There's no flaw — that logic is completely correct
The flaw is that autoencoders can't rebuild training pictures at all

exercises.explain: Great reconstruction of known points doesn't mean the model understands the space in between — that's the core gap problem!

exercises.prompt: You train a vanilla autoencoder on 1,000 handwritten digit images. It reconstructs all 1,000 training images almost perfectly. You then randomly sample a brand-new point in latent space that wasn't near any training example. What's the MOST likely result?

options.text: A blurry or garbled image that doesn't look like any real digit
A perfect new handwritten digit never seen before
An exact copy of one of the 1,000 training images
The autoencoder will crash and produce no output

exercises.explain: Since that random point falls in an unexplored gap, the decoder has no learned meaning for it — resulting in messy, garbled output rather than a clean digit.
